name: PostgreSQL protocol
description: PostgreSQL protocol is a stateful client-server database protocol that carries authentication messages, SQL commands, and result sets between front-end clients (drivers, applications) and back-end database servers. It functions as a data-flow component, transporting structured queries, parameters, and binary or textual results across a network boundary and negotiating session parameters (authentication method, protocol version, optional TLS). As a transport mechanism it handles sensitive information (usernames, authentication exchanges, query text, and returned data) but does not itself enforce application-level authorization beyond presenting authentication options. Integration points include client libraries/drivers, application servers, connection pools, network equipment (load balancers, firewalls), monitoring/logging systems, and server-side extensions. Security characteristics to consider: multiple authentication schemes (plaintext, MD5, SCRAM, GSSAPI, TLS), optional encryption, protocol-version negotiation (possible downgrade), session state that can be hijacked, and extensibility (extensions, COPY, custom functions) that expands attack surface. Trust boundaries exist at network perimeters, between application and DB tiers, and between client drivers and server endpoints. No inherited class information was provided.

name: JWT Token validation
description: The JWT Token validation component is responsible for parsing and verifying JSON Web Tokens presented by clients. It checks token headers and payload, validates cryptographic signatures against stored keys, enforces expiration and policy claims (issuer, audience, roles), and rejects tampered or expired tokens. Functioning as an authentication and authorization gatekeeper, it handles untrusted input within the server security boundary, integrates with key management or identity provider services for key rotation, and depends on secure cryptographic libraries. Misconfigurations or implementation flaws can expose protected resources or allow unauthorized access.

name: Client access to DocumentDB (MongoDB)
description: Represents the client-side access to an Amazon DocumentDB cluster using MongoDB-compatible drivers and APIs. Applications establish TCP/TLS connections to the cluster endpoint to perform read and write operations. Clients interact with primary instances for data modification and optionally with read replicas, abstracted behind a single endpoint. This data flow traverses network boundaries, requires proper authentication, encryption, and access controls. It depends on underlying driver libraries and Amazon-managed replication. Misconfigurations or vulnerabilities in drivers, network settings, or credentials can expose sensitive data or allow unauthorized actions.

name: SQS Message Queue Workflow
description: The SQS Message Queue Workflow provides a fully managed, durable message queuing service that enables asynchronous communication between distributed application components. Producers send messages to the SQS queue, which stores them reliably across multiple servers. Consumers poll the queue, temporarily hide messages during processing via visibility timeouts to avoid duplicates, and delete messages upon successful processing. This component relies on AWS IAM for access control, supports encryption at rest (SSE-SQS) and in transit (TLS), and integrates with monitoring services like CloudWatch and CloudTrail for observability. Security implications include proper policy configuration, encryption enforcement, and runtime monitoring to protect message confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

name: PayPal Payment Integration Flow
description: The PayPal Payment Integration Flow is a data flow component responsible for orchestrating RESTful HTTPS communications between an eCommerce application and PayPal’s payment gateway. It authenticates requests using OAuth 2.0 access tokens, transmits payment and order details for authorization, and processes transaction status and confirmation responses. This component handles sensitive financial and personal data in transit, enforces encryption in transit, and integrates with external services. It establishes trust boundaries at the eCommerce interface and with PayPal’s API endpoints, relying on correct OAuth token management, input validation, and secure session handling. As a critical part of the checkout pipeline, it requires robust protection against token misuse, injection attacks, and tampering to maintain confidentiality, integrity, and availability of payment transactions.

name: Token Introspection
description: Token Introspection is an OAuth 2.0 mechanism implemented as a service or API endpoint that allows resource servers and other authorized consumers to verify the validity, status, and metadata of access tokens. It processes incoming introspection requests, validates token signatures and claims, queries the authorization server or token store for token state, and returns structured metadata such as scopes, expiration, user identifiers, and associated claims. As part of the authentication and authorization workflow, it serves as a critical trust boundary between resource servers and the authorization server. Token Introspection handles sensitive data—including access tokens and user attributes—and relies on secure network channels, robust client authentication, proper input validation, and strict access controls. Misconfigurations or implementation flaws can expose token metadata, leak user information, or allow unauthorized privilege escalation across distributed services.

name: OIDC authentication
description: The OIDC authentication component is responsible for verifying user identities and managing user sessions by implementing the OpenID Connect (OIDC) protocol. It extends the OAuth 2.0 framework to provide single sign-on (SSO) capabilities and issues ID tokens, access tokens, and refresh tokens via standardized authorization flows. The component interfaces with external identity providers and internal identity stores, handling sensitive authentication data—such as user credentials and tokens—in transit (protected by TLS) and at rest (encrypted). As a critical trust boundary between external clients and internal services, it enforces password complexity, account lockout, multi-factor authentication, strict redirect URI validation, rate limiting, and generates comprehensive audit logs. Integration points include authorization and token endpoints, userinfo endpoints, and session stores, making robust encryption, input validation, access controls, and real-time monitoring essential to mitigate token theft, credential-based attacks, and session hijacking.

name: OAuth response
description: The OAuth response component is responsible for delivering OAuth tokens (access and optionally refresh tokens) along with metadata such as expiration times from the authorization server to the client application. It functions as a data flow in the OAuth protocol, encapsulating sensitive bearer tokens that grant access to protected resources. This component traverses trust boundaries between the authorization server, client application, and resource server. Security implications include the need for confidentiality and integrity of token transmissions, correct validation of token signatures, secure token storage on both client and server, and strict access control of refresh mechanisms. Misconfigurations or implementation flaws can lead to token theft, replay attacks, privilege escalation, and unauthorized resource access.

name: HTTP traffic
description: HTTP traffic is the bidirectional data flow of client requests and server responses using the Hypertext Transfer Protocol. It enables the transfer of web resources such as HTML, JSON, images, and video over a stateless request–response model. By default it transmits data in plaintext, crossing security boundaries between user agents, proxies, load balancers, and origin servers. It depends on underlying TCP/IP and DNS services and integrates with web application firewalls, caching layers, and routing infrastructure. Security characteristics include lack of inherent encryption, susceptibility to packet interception, message tampering, injection attacks, session hijacking, and denial-of-service threats unless augmented by TLS and proper access controls.

name: HTTP REST traffic
description: HTTP REST traffic is a stateless bidirectional data flow that carries client requests and server responses over HTTP using the REST architectural style. It facilitates retrieval, creation, modification, and deletion of web resources (JSON, HTML, images, video) and depends on underlying TCP/IP, DNS, and intermediary infrastructure such as proxies, load balancers, caching layers, and web application firewalls. By default it transmits data in plaintext, exposing it to interception, tampering, injection, session hijacking, replay attacks, and denial-of-service without additional protections. Proper mitigation requires TLS encryption, strict input validation, authentication, authorization, and comprehensive logging and monitoring.

name: Log ingestion
description: Log ingestion is a managed logging ingestion service that accepts log events from external producers (applications, EC2 instances, containers, serverless functions, agents) via an API or agent protocol, authenticates and validates incoming events, organizes them into logical containers (log groups and streams), persists them in a managed data store, and forwards exports or subscription streams to downstream consumers (dashboards, alerting, S3, Kinesis, Lambda). It handles both structured and unstructured log data, supports retention and export rules, and integrates with identity and access controls, encryption services (customer or provider-managed keys), network controls (VPC endpoints, endpoints policies), and monitoring/alerting stacks. Security characteristics: AWS-managed components reduce operational burden but retain shared-responsibility risks (configuration, IAM, key management, exports). Trust boundaries exist between log producers, the ingestion API endpoint, and downstream consumers; misconfigurations at any boundary can cause data exposure, integrity loss, or availability impacts. Integration points and dependencies include client agents/SDKs, IAM roles/policies, network (public endpoints, VPC endpoints), KMS keys for encryption, destination services (S3/Kinesis/Lambda), CloudWatch Logs Insights, and audit trails (CloudTrail). No inherited class information provided.

name: User auth Customer authentication
description: The customer authentication component is responsible for verifying user identities using credentials such as passwords, PINs, or biometric data. It processes login requests, enforces password complexity and account lockout policies, supports multi-factor authentication, and issues secure session tokens. This component interfaces with identity stores or external identity providers, handles sensitive authentication data in transit and at rest, and generates audit logs for all authentication events. It represents a critical trust boundary between external users and internal services, requiring robust encryption, access controls, rate limiting, and real-time monitoring to mitigate unauthorized access and credential-based attacks.
